data.title: Cobalt Miners News For The Month Of April 2026
data.description:
Welcome to the April 2026 cobalt miners news.

The past month saw cobalt prices flat.

Cobalt price news

As of April 21, the cobalt spot price was at US$25.53/lb, flat from US$25.53/lb last month. The LME cobalt price is US$55,350/tonne. LME Cobalt inventory is 105 tonnes, down from 121 tonnes last month. More details on cobalt pricing (in particular the more relevant cobalt sulphate), can be found here at Benchmark Mineral Intelligence or Fast Markets MB.

Cobalt spot price long term chart - USD 25.53/lb (source)

Cobalt demand v supply forecasts

The new DRC export quota system has likely flipped the ex-DRC market balance from a cobalt surplus to a deficit in 2026 (RHS chart) (source)

Trend Investing v IEA 2021 demand forecast for EV metals (Trend Investing) (IEA)

Cobalt market news

On April 9 Fastmarkets reported:

DRC quota extension eases cobalt supply concerns, but market reaction muted...DRC’s export deadline extension may ease near-term supply pressure, but logistics remain the bottleneck, with shipments slower than expected and operational constraints limiting the immediate flow of cobalt to the market. On Tuesday, the DRC added another 30 days to the deadline for exporting volumes allocated to miners for the fourth quarter of 2025 – which had already been rolled over to the end of the first quarter of 2026 – under the country’s new quota system. Market participants said shipments from the DRC have so far been slower than expected, prompting the authorities to adjust the policy...“With relatively little material reportedly being shipped from the DRC, it was necessary for the government to act; otherwise, supply would have been even tighter,” he said.

On April 14 S&P Global announced:

Asian cobalt market faces tight Q2 amid supply concerns. DRC export delays tighten cobalt supply. Chinese cobalt hydroxide imports plunge 95%. Weak NMC battery demand limits price growth.

On April 16 Reuters reported:

Congo creates strategic cobalt reserve to influence supply and prices, regulator says.

  • Strategic reserve enables Congo to influence global cobalt supply and market prices.
  • 10% of cobalt export quotas allocated to strategic reserve, unused quotas forfeited to state.
  • Minerals regulator ARECOMS given authority to acquire, hold ​and market strategic minerals.

On April 17 Yahoo Finance reported:

$34.73 Bn cobalt market trends and future forecasts to 2035: Cobalt sulfate leads market segments; Industrial chemicals and aerospace sectors to accelerate growth...The global cobalt market is projected to expand from USD 16.12 billion in the current year to USD 34.73 billion by 2035, showcasing a CAGR of 7.23% over the forecast period.

The global cobalt market is projected to expand from USD 16.12B in 2025 to USD 34.73B by 2035, at a CAGR of 7.23% (source)

Cobalt company news

CMOC Group Limited [HKSE:3993] [SHE:603993] (CMCLF) (formerly China Molybdenum)

No cobalt news for the month.

Glencore [HK:805] [LSE:GLEN] (GLCNF) - MOU with Orion Critical Minerals Consortium ("Orion CMC") to sell 40% of its DRC assets to Orion CMC (a US Gov. backed consortium)

No cobalt news; however there was some Aluminum related news.

Zheijiang Huayou Cobalt [SHA:603799] - DRC subsidiary Congo Dongfang International Mining

On April 28 Huayou Cobalt announced:

2025 Annual Shareholders’ Meeting...During the reporting period, the Company achieved operating revenue of RMB 81.019 billion, representing a year-on-year growth of 32.94%, achieved a net profit attributable to the parent company of RMB 6.110 billion, a year-on-year increase of 47.07%, a significant rise compared to the previous year, setting a new record for the best operating performance in history...

Jinchuan Group International Resources [HK:2362]

On March 31 Market Screener reported: "Jinchuan Group International Resources posts FY profit of $38.2 million." Profit was for calendar year 2025 and in USD. Full details here.

Note: On March 15 Jinchuan gave an update on the investigation and continued suspension of trading. Shares remain suspended from trading.

GEM Co Ltd [SHE:002340]

On April 22 Market Screener reported: "GEM's attributable profit rises 55% in 2025." Full details here.

Virtus Minerals (private) and a US led consortium (details here) - Took over Chemaf

On March 31 the WSJ reported:

In win for Trump, U.S. firm acquires huge Congolese cobalt miner. Virtus Minerals buys one of the few major cobalt producers not already under Chinese control.

Eurasian Resources Group ("ERG") - private

ERG own the Metalkol facility in the DRC where ERG processes cobalt and copper tailings with a capacity of up to 24,000 tonnes of cobalt pa.

On April 20 Reuters reported: "ERG expects limited Congo cobalt hydroxide output recovery after deliberate 2025 cut."

Umicore SA [Brussels:UMI] (UMICY)

On March 31 Umicore announced: "Umicore opens innovation center in Shanghai."

Sumitomo Metal Mining Co. (TYO:5713) (STMNF)("SMM")

On April 1 SMM announced: "FY2026 Metal Production Plan." No details on cobalt.

On April 7 Market Screener reported: "Japan's Sumitomo Metal sees FY2026/27 copper output rising 3%."

MMC Norilsk Nickel ("Nornickel")

No cobalt news for the month.

Sherritt International [TSX:S] (SHERF)

On April 7 Sherritt International announced: "Closing of non brokered Private Placement...raising total gross proceeds of approximately $43.5 million."

Nickel 28 [TSXV:NKL] [GR:3JC] (CONXF)

Nickel 28 holds an 8.56% JV interest in Ramu which is operated by the Metallurgical Corporation of China (“MCC”).

On April 15 Nickel 28 announced:

Proposed Ramu expansion and related developments...it has been advised by Ramu NiCo Management (MCC) Limited, the operator of the Ramu Nickel-Cobalt integrated operation in Papua New Guinea (the "Ramu Operation"), that a proposal for development has been lodged with the Mineral Resources Authority of Papua New Guinea under the Mining Act, detailing a proposed phase II expansion of the Ramu Operation...the Proposed Expansion would be expected to approximately double the production capacity of the current Ramu Operation at a projected cost of approximately US$1.6 billion...the Company (Nickel 28) is currently analyzing and evaluating its various options afforded to it under the Joint Venture Agreements.

Electra Battery Materials [TSXV:ELBM] (ELBM) ("Electra")

On March 27 Electra announced:

Electra restarts construction and reports 2025 Financial Results. Board approved US$73 million construction budget to complete the refinery... Early commissioning activities are expected to begin in the fourth quarter of 2026, with mechanical completion targeted for the second quarter of 2027 and commercial production anticipated in the fourth quarter of 2027.

On April 9 Electra announced: "Electra awards $7.8 Million in construction contracts for Refinery infrastructure."

Other smaller cobalt producers

  • Vale SA (VALE)
  • Zijin Mining Group [SSE:601899]
  • China Nonferrous Metal Mining Company (CNMC)
  • Jiangsu Cobalt
  • Nanjing Hanrui Cobalt
  • Pengxin International Mining Co
  • Zijin Mining Group [SSE:601899]
  • Wanbau
  • GTL
  • Jiana Energy
  • Sicomes
  • Shenzhen GEM High-Tech Co

Possible mid-term producers (2027 onwards)

Sunrise Energy Metals Limited [ASX:SRL] (SREMF) ("Sunrise")

Sunrise Energy Metals has 132kt contained cobalt at their Sunrise Project. They also own the Syerston Scandium Project.

On April 21 Sunrise announced:

Quarterly Activities Report...Until market conditions improve, activities at the Sunrise Battery Materials Complex (Ni-Co) remain focussed on minimising holdings costs and preserving project optionality.

Ardea Resources [ASX:ARL] (ARRRF)

In total, Ardea has 6.1Mt of contained nickel and 386,000t of contained cobalt at their KNP Project near Kalgoorlie in Western Australia. The Goongarrie Hub, within the KNP, hosts 584Mt at 0.69% nickel and 0.043% cobalt for 4Mt of contained nickel and 250kt of contained cobalt. Ardea is also exploring for gold and nickel sulphide on their >5,100 km2 of 100% controlled tenements in the Eastern Goldfields region of Western Australia.

On March 31 Ardea Resources announced:

Kalgoorlie Nickel Project – Goongarrie Hub DFS update. Strong progress continues to be made on completing the DFS, with key workstreams advancing across mining and tailings, process definition, testwork, water and infrastructure, approvals and risk. The Consortium has earned an aggregate holding in KNPL of 35% under the terms of the Cooperation Agreement.

On April 9 Ardea Resources announced:

Kalgoorlie Nickel Project beneficiary of Federal Government Investor Front Door Program (IFD)...IFD support provides dedicated Federal Government engagement to streamline multi‑agency approvals and improve regulatory certainty, over and above that already granted through the Major Project Status...Facilitates access to government financing pathways...

Cobalt Blue Holdings [ASX:COB] (CBBHF)

Cobalt Blue has 87kt of contained cobalt at their 100% owned Broken Hill Cobalt Project [BHCP] in NSW, Australia. COB also has an earn-in agreement (earn in to 51% up to 75%) for the Halls Creek Project in WA (copper-lead-zinc-silver-gold). They are also developing the Kwinana Cobalt Refinery.

On March 30 Cobalt Blue announced:

Cobalt Blue and Glomar Minerals partner on nodule processing. Consortium aims to build processing facility in the U.S...The venture combines Glomar Minerals’ licenses and offshore minerals extraction capabilities with Cobalt Blue’s proven expertise in critical minerals processing. Polymetallic nodules provide an abundant, multi‑metal resource containing cobalt (Co), manganese (Mn), nickel (Ni), copper (Cu), iron (Fe), rare earth elements and titanium (Ti), enabling multi‑commodity production from a single feedstock source. The consortium will soon progress feasibility studies for the processing of polymetallic nodules, including piloting testwork at Cobalt Blue’s Broken Hill Technology Centre. At the same time, development planning is underway for a proposed “world-first” processing facility in the United States, with a targeted capacity of approximately 200,000 tonnes per annum.

On April 20 Cobalt Blue announced: "Cobalt Blue featured on 7NEWS Spotlight." Access to the 7 News video here.

Australian Mines [ASX:AUZ] (AMSLF)

Australian Mines own the Sconi nickel-cobalt Project in Queensland Australia, the Flemington scandium–nickel–cobalt Project in NSW Australia, and the Boa Vista Gold Project in Brazil.

No significant news for the month.

Havilah Resources [ASX:HAV] [GR:FWL] (HAVRF)

Havilah 100% own the Mutooroo copper-cobalt project about 60km west of Broken Hill in South Australia. They also have the nearby Kalkaroo copper-gold-cobalt JV Project (20% Havilah, 80% agreement with Sandfire Resources), as well as a potentially large iron ore project at Grants. The Kalkaroo copper-gold-cobalt deposit contains JORC Mineral Resources of 1.1 million tonnes of copper, 3.1 million ounces of gold and 23,200 tonnes of cobalt.

No news for the month.

Alliance Nickel Limited [ASX:AXN] (GMRSF)

Alliance Nickel own the NiWest Nickel-Cobalt Project located adjacent to Glencore’s Murrin Murrin Nickel operations in the North Eastern Goldfields of Western Australia. The NiWest Project which has an estimated 830,000 tonnes of nickel metal and 52,000 tonnes of cobalt.

On April 2 Alliance Nickel announced:

Entitlement Offer. 1-for-6.42 Non-renounceable Entitlement Offer to raise approximately A$4.15 million1 at an issue price of $0.035 per New Share.

Global Energy Metals Corp. [TSXV:GEMC][GR:5GE1] (GBLEF) ("GEMC")

On April 21 GEMC announced: "Global Energy Metals and Zimtu Capital Corp. agree to terminate Option Agreement for the Monument Peak Project, Idaho."

Giga Metals Corp. [TSXV:GIGA][FSE: BRR2] (GIGGF) Turnagain Nickel-Cobalt Project is now held via the JV company Hard Creek Nickel Corporation [TSXV:HNC] (HNCKF) (85% Giga Metals: 15% Mitsubishi Corp.)

On April 15 Giga Metals announced: "Non-Brokered Private Placement...for aggregate gross proceeds of up to C$1,000,000, at a price of $0.08 per Unit."

The Metals Company (TMC) ("TMC")

TMC state: "We produce base metals from polymetallic nodules, providing the raw materials for energy, infrastructure and manufacturing. For over a decade, we’ve been exploring the planet’s largest known deposit of battery-grade metals: nodules on the seafloor of the Clarion Clipperton Zone in the Pacific Ocean."

On March 27 TMC announced: "Fourth Quarter and Full Year 2025 results... Total cash of approximately $117.6 million at December 31, 2025."

On April 16 TMC announced:

TMC subsidiaries submit massive deep-sea dataset to public database as Company launches video series on findings of environmental research.

Chilean Cobalt Corp. (OTCQB:COBA)

Chilean Cobalt Corp. (“C3”) is a critical minerals exploration and development company focused on the La Cobaltera Project located in Chile’s historic San Juan cobalt district.

On April 1 Chilean Cobalt announced:

Chilean Cobalt Corp. Board of Directors approves Governance Framework. The initiative also strengthens the Company's positioning as it advances discussions related to its $317 million non‑binding Letter of Interest from the Export‑Import Bank of the United States (EXIM).

Note: There was also some Chilean Cobalt rare earths news you can read here.

Conclusion

April saw cobalt spot prices flat over the past month.

Highlights for the month were:

  • DRC’s export deadline extension may ease near-term supply pressure.
  • Asian cobalt market faces tight Q2 amid supply concerns. DRC export delays tighten cobalt supply. Weak NMC battery demand limits price growth.
  • Congo creates strategic cobalt reserve to influence supply and prices.
  • Cobalt market trends to 2035 - Industrial chemicals and aerospace sectors to accelerate growth. The global cobalt market is projected to expand from USD 16.12B in 2025 to USD 34.73B by 2035, at a CAGR of 7.23%.
  • Huayou Cobalt 2025 net profit surges by 47% YoY.
  • Jinchuan Group International Resources reports 2025 net profit of US$38.2 million, following a loss in 2024.
  • GEM Co's attributable profit rises 55% in 2025.
  • Sherritt Private Placement raises approximately $43.5 million.
  • Nickel 28 - Ramu NiCo Management proposed expansion to double the production capacity of the current Ramu Operation.
  • Electra awards $7.8M in construction contracts for Refinery infrastructure. Targets Q4 2027 for commercial production.
  • Kalgoorlie Nickel Project beneficiary of Federal Government Investor Front Door Program.
  • Cobalt Blue and Glomar Minerals partner on nodule processing. Consortium aims to build processing facility in the U.S.
  • TMC subsidiaries submit massive deep-sea dataset to public database.

As usual all comments are welcome.

data.title: Alkane Resources: Worth Getting Into Despite An Already Massive 2026 Rally
data.description:
Note: Alkane reports all its figures in AUD. For convenience, asset prices for gold/antimony have been mentioned in USD, and price targets in the listing currency.

Alkane Resources Ltd. (ALKEF) (ALK:CA) is an established mid-tier gold and antimony producer from Australia. In 2025 it entered into a merger of equals with Canada-based Mandalay Resources, following which it has three main producing mines: Tomingley (the company’s cornerstone gold asset in New South Wales) and the Costerfield mine in Australia and the Björkdal mine in Sweden, acquired after the merger, which added high-producing antimony assets to their portfolio.

The biggest story in Alkane right now is their massive Boda-Kaiser discovery. Their geological team, led by Ian Chalmers and David Meates, hypothesized in 2016 that Macquire Arc (which houses Newmont’s Cadia-Ridgeway) should continue up north, and they were able to confirm a massive greenfield gold-copper deposit in 2019 in one of the most stunning finds in modern Australian mining. A scoping study, however, showed it would need nearly AUD $1.78 billion in capex, leading eventually to the merger with Mandalay Resources, which had cash generators (Costerfield and Björkdal) but lacked a generational asset.

Such ambition is common enough in established mid-sized players, but the story is usually one of free cash flow looking for a project. This is a rare instance of an exciting new discovery in the hands of established players that know how to execute.

Valuing the producing mines

As of June 30, Alkane’s proven and probable gold reserves were 621,000 ounces at Tomingley, 150,000 ounces at Costerfield and 543,000 ounces at Björkdal – amounting a total of 1,314,000 ounces. By March 2026, Alkane had already produced 122,963 ounces of gold since the start of FY 2026 (i.e., July 1, 2025). This means that across its three main sites, there are approximately 1.2 million ounces of proven and probable reserves of gold (not including inferred resources). Costerfield also holds 10,700 tonnes of antimony reserves, of which 768 tonnes have been mined in FY 26 so far.

The technical report published in June 2025 pegged the pre-tax NPV at around AUD $130 million (pg. 165; valuation at 8% discount, with gold at USD $1,950/ounce). Adjusting up for a new consensus price of gold at USD $3000 per ounce and taking into account the depletion of reserves and increasing royalties and diesel costs pushing the AISC up from AUD $2,100 to AUD $2,400, we get a scaled pre-tax NPV of AUD $880 million.

FY 2026 financial guidance pegged Björkdal’s AISC at much higher than the group average, at AUD $4,100 - with proven & probable reserves of 543 kilo ounces, with a depletion factor of 0.94 and gold at USD $3,000, the scaled pre-tax NPV comes to AUD $152 million.

For Costerfield, the AISC was AUD $2,400, and we also have to consider the massive increase in antimony prices from USD $12,000 per tonne to USD $35,000 per tonne. With 150 kilo ounces of gold and 10 kilo tonnes of antimony providing a massive tailwind, this gives us a scaled pre-tax NPV of over AUD $600 million.

Combined, the proven and probable reserves of Alkane’s 3 primary producing mines have a combined NPV of roughly AUD $1.65 billion. Add to this the free cash reserves of AUD $370, and we get a total ballpark of around AUD $2 billion (~USD $1.42 billion). The company's current market cap is ~ USD $1.6 billion. This is a factor of 1.12x and means that the market is currently pricing in the non-reserve resources in the 3 main mines, the massive Boda-Kaiser development project, as well as the Kendal deposit in Costerfield, the Nagambie earn-in transaction, and a number of other growth projects, between AUD $200-300 million, at best.

Valuing the growth

Resource expansion of the existing mines during the 2025-26 drilling season has been significant. The McLean and Plains extensions at Tomingley have seen successful interceptions, which are likely to be added to the mine plans in the June 2026 update. The Boda South and Driell prospects have shown mineralization at depth and continuation of the Boda footprint beyond that included in the Scoping Study. They are also applying the technical knowledge gained at Costerfield (rare mining know-how of narrow-vein gold-antimony deposits) to a new, underexplored area in Victoria through the earn-in deal with Nagambie resources.

Now, it is understandably difficult to attribute value to these exploratory projects, but it’s also not entirely sensible to value them at zero. We look instead at comparable deposits. For instance, Boda is in the Macquarie Arc—the same geological belt as Newmont’s world-class Cadia-Ridgeway. If preliminary studies confirm that Boda shares geology, the inferred reserves in a Tier-1 province tend to get valued at a 20-30% premium on the implications of scale alone. Meanwhile at Tomingley, the McLean prospect has given gold intercepts of 4.3 g/t (including very high ‘jewellery boxes’ of up to 22.8 g/t), significantly higher than the average mine grade, which is up to 2.5 g/t. Being adjacent to the existing facilities, this is low-hanging fruit, and along with some deep-drilling success at El Paso, we should see some initial numbers in the June 2026 update. I think it’s reasonable to factor in an AUD $50-100 million addition in value through all such extensions cumulatively.

Meanwhile, the greenfield Boda-Kaiser project remains their largest find, discovered in November 2019, with the Scoping Study revealing 8.3 million ounces of gold and 1.5 million tonnes of copper resources. Located in New South Wales’ Macquarie Arc, it shares the same geology as Newmont’s world-class Cadia-Ridgeway, with an annual production potential of 225,000 ounces of gold equivalent. The scoping study (essentially the Australian terminology for Preliminary Economic Assessment, or PEA) pegs the final NPV of this AUD $1.8 billion (gold at USD $2,345 and copper at USD $10,050). If we take consensus gold at USD $3,000 and leave copper untouched (to accommodate increasing AISC, etc.), we get a scaled-up NPV of over AUD $3 billion. A margin of error for PEAs is usually up to 35%, so let’s scale this down by that much to approx. AUD $1.95 billion. The study has 85% indicated reserves, as opposed to the usual 50-70%. Taking a conservative reserves-to-resource conversion ratio at 40%, we get an NPV of around AUD $780 million. If we include a (very minimal) value addition of around $20 million from various extensions and projects mentioned above, we can round off the figure to AUD $800 million.

This means that, scaling up for new asset prices but sticking to extremely conservative estimates and rounding down at almost every step, we get a value for the growth assets of around AUD $800 million, which the market is currently only valuing at AUD $200-300 million. Understandably, permitting and execution risk, especially for the Boda-Kaiser project, still remain to be overcome. For an institution, then, it perhaps makes sense to not jump in at this level. For any individual with a moderate risk appetite, however, this is a fantastically discounted valuation.

The stock is basically pricing in the existing reserves fully, and pricing the incredible growth potential from reserves and the new discovery at 0.2-0.3x P/NPV. That’s slightly worse than what junior miners sitting on such an asset at this stage would get. Alkane isn’t a junior miner. A new discovery in the hands of a seasoned miner, that too in a Tier-1 jurisdiction, has vastly improved permitting and execution risks and, ideally, and conservatively, demands a 0.5x-0.6x P/NPV in my opinion. That means that, as of today, there should be at least an additional market cap of AUD $300 million if the risk was fairly valued. That would take the total market cap to AUD $2.6 billion.

That means that Alkane should be conservatively priced about ~15% higher based on just the known numbers right now—ignoring the high probability that massive projects like Boda-Kaiser are likely to reveal more deposits as studies progress.

Price targets on TSX (ALK) and US OTC (ALKEF)

The above means that on the TSX, Alkane (ALK:CA) should be priced at CAD $1.82—which is exactly where it was about a week ago before pulling back sharply, mainly on profit taking after its inclusion on the ASX 200. It also trades on the pink tier of OTC markets in the US (ALKEF), where it has taken a dip from what should be its base level of USD $1.33 towards USD $1.18.

This recent downtick is a temporary and marginal one compared to what the company has seen in 2026, more than doubling in price. I have taken extremely conservative numbers in this article above to try and arrive at a ‘base level’ if the stock was to trade without any growth multiple—the stock right now is ~15% under that base level. I think it’s highly probable that the 2026 growth spurt is far from over, and given how good it looks, a decent growth multiple is to be expected, taking ALK over CAD $2 / ALKEF over USD $1.5 for a 30-35% upside this year, especially with progress on funding, and the June 2026 report on mineral resources.

Risks

The capital requirement of AUD $1.79 billion remains the biggest hurdle for the moment in the development of the new discovery. They are currently aggressively building up liquidity—adding AUD $128 million in just 90 days. With the Final Investment Decision on Boda-Kaiser scheduled almost half a decade away, Alkane seems to be hoping that riding the gold super-cycle will allow it to milk its three mines enough to create self-sufficiency in funding the project, failing which the next best hope will be to get a major strategic partner on board, such as Newmont (which is already involved in that geology) or Rio Tinto. The situation does, however, remain speculative for the time being—with the worst-case scenario being equity dilution.

Besides this, there are also other usual risks of resource-reserve conversion (which June should provide better clarity on), antimony price risk (since antimony is heavily dependent on Chinese supply), and creeping costs and margin compression. All this means that even if we give the benefit of the doubt to Alkane over permitting and executing—given their experience and the Tier-1 jurisdictions they operate in—there is a significant fog of war that will take between 6 and 18 months to clear up. So despite the enormous possibilities, I would be reluctant to recommend this as a wealth-builder just yet (except for sophisticated investors with the requisite risk appetite). But as outlined above, even factoring in these risks, a 15-20% upside over the next 2-3 months is due in this stock and creates a better-defined play for traders.

Conclusion

Despite its recent rally, Alkane offers an incredibly soft cushion with limited downside. Its current level doesn’t entirely reflect its present value, let alone its future potential—and this gap can be played for a further 15-25% upside this year. For long-term speculators, Alkane could be a big wealth generator. But that requires clearing two main hurdles first. The Feasibility Study should provide some clarity on the extent and profitability of the reserves (though the geology is very promising), and it is likely that it will take a few sustained quarters of cash flow to get clarity on whether Alkane will be in a position to self-fund the new mine, or will require partnerships. Whether or not they have the spare cash and risk appetite to get in early, mining investors will definitely want to track this one over the next 18 months or so to see where this story is headed.

data.title: General Mills: GLP-1 And Consumer Fears Are Overblown (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Back when I last covered General Mills (GIS), I reiterated their Buy rating despite near-term headwinds while the company focused on cost savings and growth reinvestments.

With the stock down significantly from that level and the company continuing its turnaround efforts while still paying a very solid yield, GIS is upgraded to Strong Buy, with risks looking priced in and a strong margin of safety implied at current levels.

Turnaround Advances in a Tough Environment

GIS' Q3'FY26 report missed the market's top- and bottom-line estimates, reporting a 3% drop in organic net sales and a 32% and 37% drop in Adj. Operating Profit and EPS, respectively.

However, the company is expecting the timing headwinds they saw so far during the fiscal year to turn into tailwinds for Q4, basically normalizing and highlighting why it's important to look at results from a longer perspective than just one quarter, although this miss also highlights the ongoing consumer weakness (a trend visible especially in North American Retail and Foodservice), as highlighted by other analysts.

Although it may be counterintuitive to see what's traditionally considered a defensive business perform like this during a bad environment, we're talking about a trend that's very common (and actually natural) in the sector over the past few years, as I'll detail below.

General Mills also reaffirmed its guidance, expecting organic net sales to drop by 1.5% to 2% alongside a 16% to 20% drop in Adj. Diluted EPS and a 95% FCF conversion rate (based on the adjusted after-tax earnings), which should result in an FCF of about $1.85 billion in FY26, although they expect FY27 to mark their comeback after a year focused on business adjustments (including the yogurt and Brazil businesses sale) and brand investments, all while expecting $600 million in combined savings in FY26 thanks to their global transformation.

Financially, based on GIS' latest report, we continue to see a good position overall, with the current assets not covering their current liabilities due to their payable-receivable balance, but the debt remains manageable, allowing them to focus on shareholder returns while improving the balance sheet.

Back in early April 2026, General Mills also announced issuing euro bonds, with €1 billion 4.750% Series A fixed-to-fixed reset rate junior subordinated notes and €700 million 5.250% Series B notes, both due in 2056, which will be used to repay their €250 million floating rate senior notes due April 2026, $750 million 3.20% notes due February 2027, and outstanding commercial paper.

Regarding that, alongside debt reductions, GIS returned $987.2 million in dividends (annualized yield of nearly 7%) and $500.3 million through buybacks. Annualized, the dividend should represent about $1.30 billion, which remains well covered by their free cash flow even if we assume no growth or even a hit in the near-term as they focus on portfolio improvements.

They don't have to cut it since it's sustained by their strong cash flow even today, but I'd rather see them focus on buybacks at least while prices are around current levels, while the company and its predecessor have paid a dividend without interruption for 127 years.

Industry's Fundamentals Are Shifting

Although this industry may seem defensive given its traditional image in the market, reality proves that this is far from the truth, with Deutsche Bank analyst Steve Powers and his team recently pointing out several factors that impact the CPG industry and question the "defensive play" idea.

These are trends that go well beyond the classic tariffs/Iran pressure, such as growing GLP-1 adoption, private label brands, aging demographics, and the rising K-shaped economy and its gaps, among others that I also described before.

However, I'd also like to address how the GLP-1 concerns may be overblown when it comes to this industry. Yes, it's a massive catalyst/tailwind for companies like Novo Nordisk (NVO), but the impact on GIS' and its industry is not that big based on the current studies, with a recent one from Morgan Stanley expecting about 15% of the US population to use GLP-1s by 2035.

In other words, even if we see the worst effects affecting GIS' portfolio, that's still a 1-2% decline in terms of volume if we look at the findings of other studies, which indicate hits between 6.7% to 11.1% in terms of grocery spending for categories such as chips, sweet bakery, side dishes, or cookies - and that's over a 10-year period (adoption will likely vary based on the country), giving plenty of time for these companies to adjust their pricing/portfolio.

At least when it comes to the near term, the ongoing Iran conflict will very likely be the most influential factor for the industry, as the region plays a key role in commodities that play a vital role in the global economy, and the damage that's already been done to its infrastructure can take months or even years to repair, leading to expectations of persisting war premiums for much longer than the conflict itself.

As a result, since rate hikes are a main way in which central banks can combat inflation, it wouldn't be impossible to see the Fed consider hiking at the very least pausing the cuts, regardless of who the Fed Chair is, as that's still just 1 out of 12 votes in the FOMC, so it doesn't really matter that Powell's term as Fed Chair ends in May 2026, with Warsh (or whoever will get all the approvals needed) likely having a very rough start to his mandate.

This can further pressure the already weak consumer environment, potentially leading to even more trade-downs amid lower discretionary budgets, which can be a significant threat for GIS because it can push even more people towards their competitors.

Over the longer-term, however, competition from private/white labels is probably the most important headwind to pay attention to, especially since the trade-downs and consumer weakness were a perfect storm that allowed major retailers to capitalize on these brands.

Not only that, but given the significant amounts of data and improved brand image and trust since more people were pushed into trying private label brands, these retailers can also capture the upside by launching higher-quality brands under these names, taking advantage of the newfound brand power and affecting legacy brands.

This one is likely the biggest fundamental threat to the industry, potentially reshaping the landscape significantly over the long term given the risk of seeing margin pressure, weaker volumes, and potential portfolio adjustments as a result.

Sure, it's also important to note that GIS and its legacy peers are not just going to sit and watch their market shares fade. Many of these companies have been actively adjusting their portfolios to consumer trends, refreshing or launching new product lines, expanding through high-protein, low-calorie, etc. variants, M&A activity, or even divesting underperforming ones to focus on their core. This adaptability is something that the company's CEO also talked about during their Q3'FY26 Earnings Call:

On Remarkable Packaging Design, we're winning at the shelf by providing options that give consumers genuine choice. This includes doubling our sales from price pack architecture initiatives from entry price points for wallets are stretched, to small sizes of the deliver for GLP-1 diets, to more premium trade-up formats for consumers seeking value beyond just price. For example, our Chex Mix tubs have been more than 60% incremental to our salty snacks business. On Brand Communication, we increased investment behind stronger creative ideas, and our media ROIs are up double digits on our biggest brand this year, including both Cheerios and Cinnamon Toast Crunch.

Also, if anything, this can first squeeze out the smaller brands that don't have billion-dollar giants behind them, and it doesn't mean people will only buy Great Value (WMT) going forward.

Risks Reiterated

Although the company should have a lot of time to adjust to the structural headwinds, we should always account for the risk of bad decisions and significant competitive pressure.

Especially if the weak consumer environment persists for longer, that's even more pressure on GIS that can also strengthen its retail competitors, potentially accelerating one of the industry's biggest threats.

Valuation

For GIS' DCF to Equity valuation, starting from an FCF of $1.85 billion as the base in FY26, I'll assume this can recover to $2 billion in FY27 thanks to their ongoing portfolio optimizations, cost savings, and expected volume improvements, followed by a 4.5% CAGR until FY30, which should be conservative, especially if we take into account the effect of buybacks on a per-share basis, supported by potential macro improvements, followed by a 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate.

Given the macro risks and potential for fundamental pressure, I'll assume an elevated discount rate of 12% to demand a strong margin of safety, with 533.7 million shares outstanding as of filing their Q3'FY26 report.

This returns an equity value of $22.76 billion, for an intrinsic value of $42.65 per share, which is above the current $35.49 at the moment of writing this despite using a high discount rate and relatively conservative estimates.

In other words, the current levels assume even more conservative levels and imply a very solid margin of safety given the 12% discount rate applied, with plenty of room for a re-rating if their turnaround advances well.

Conclusion

GIS is upgraded to Strong Buy, backed by a solid business, attractive yield, and significant discount, with potential catalysts coming from their internal moves and potential macro improvements that can unlock this value.

There are risks to keep an eye on given the industry's potential fundamental shifts, but the current valuation seems to account for a very significant margin of safety already.

data.title: Thermo Fisher Scientific: Why A Slow Q1 Does Not Reflect The Growth Opportunities
data.description:
What The Market Did Not Like About Q1

It has been six months since I last covered Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO) and downgraded the stock to a Hold rating. That looked too conservative for a little while as the stock climbed to a 4-year high above $640 in January but moved generally lower since Q4 results. At that time, the company projected 4-6% total sales growth in 2026, but just 3-4% organic sales growth. This was likely disappointing relative to Thermo's medium-term organic growth targets, which are now being talked about as in the 5-6% range, "eventually" getting to "7% plus", a bit of a hedge from the 7-9% discussed at the 2024 Investor Day.

After several flat years following the strong growth in 2020-2021 from COVID-19 testing and vaccine manufacturing demand, it appears the market is looking to get back to growth and punishing the stock when the growth is deferred. That likely factored into the over 18% drop since my last article and 28% drop since the January high.

A big chunk of this drop came in one day following the Q1 2026 earnings release. The company already warned during the Q4 earnings call that Q1 sales growth would be "a couple points lower" than the full-year forecast. So, the greater than 10% intraday drop after the earnings release seems excessive.

The headline numbers were not bad. Thermo reported sales of $11 billion and non-GAAP EPS of $5.44, both of which beat expectations. The guidance, which came out during the earnings call, was also positive. The company raised revenue guidance to $47.3 - $48.1 billion (6-8% growth over 2025, including 3-4% organic growth) and raised EPS guidance to $24.64-$25.12. At the midpoint, that is up $0.37 from the previous guidance. The operating margin for the year is forecasted to expand 70 basis points over 2025. The inorganic growth isn't anything to dismiss, though. Since my last article, Thermo has had two full quarters of owning the Purification and Filtration business, which it bought from Solventum for $4 billion. It also just closed the $9 billion purchase of Clario at the end of March. The Clario deal closed ahead of schedule and was responsible for $0.32 of the $0.37 EPS guidance raise.

Still, the Q1 results show that most of the organic growth is ahead. The 6% actual sales growth was helped by 3% from acquisitions and 2% from foreign exchange. Organic sales growth for the company was just 1%. Breaking it down by segment, the Analytical Instruments and Specialty Diagnostics segments had negative growth, Life Sciences Solutions was in line with the company, and only Lab Products and Services, the lowest-margin segment in the company, had healthy organic growth at 4%.

This low organic growth appears to be the main disappointing result from the quarter, but again, the company already warned that Q1 would be "a couple of points" below the full-year forecast.

It was already known that Fiscal Q1 would have one less day in 2026 compared to 2025. 87 days this year vs. 88 last year accounts for 1 percentage point of the lower growth. Another percentage point came from the timing of some pharma services sales. So without these factors, organic growth would have been closer to 3%. Additionally, Q1 2025 was the last quarter before the "Liberation Day" tariff roll-out. As the tariffs start annualizing in Q2 and beyond, year-on-year comparisons of margins and operating income should be easier.

There were also a couple of persistent business headwinds that impacted the negative-growth segments in Q1. First, government and academic spending was down in both the US and China, impacting mainly the analytical instruments segment. In the US, this was not nearly as bad as it could have been, as funding for the National Institutes of Health (NIH) actually increased from 2025, as opposed to a 40% cut initially proposed by the administration. Second, in the Specialty Diagnostics segment, the flu and respiratory disease season was less severe in the US than in previous years. Both of these issues are worth watching but should not impact the long-term strategy or performance for Thermo.

The big negative response to Q1 seems like an overreaction given that the headwinds were either known or not significant to long-term strategy. There are a couple of tailwinds that should help growth over the medium to long term. These are the reshoring of drug manufacturing and the use of AI in research.

Strategic Tailwinds For Growth

Supply chain issues and national security concerns about the wisdom of relying on other countries for drug manufacturing arose after the COVID-19 pandemic. The Trump administration has taken steps to incentivize reshoring of drug manufacturing in the US. On April 2, the administration rolled out 100% tariffs on patented pharmaceuticals and their ingredients under Section 232. These tariffs are set to take effect after 120 days for large companies and 180 days for smaller companies. There are several ways to secure partial exemptions from these tariffs. Companies that enter into Most Favored Nation pricing and onshoring agreements with the administration are exempt from the tariff through 2029. Companies that sign onshoring agreements only can have the tariff reduced to 20%. Also, certain countries and trade areas that already signed a trade agreement with the US will have drug imports tariffed at those rates.

Despite the exemptions, there is still plenty of interest in reshoring drug manufacturing. Large companies have discussed plans for as much as $480 billion of investments in domestic drug manufacturing capacity. Think Global Health estimates that 15% of this capex could be directed toward manufacturing equipment that is right in the wheelhouse of Thermo Fisher. That would mean a TAM of $72 million with Thermo well positioned as one of the largest producers of this equipment.

For smaller companies or those that want to get up to speed more quickly on manufacturing in the US, Thermo's Lab Products and Services segment includes Contract Development and Manufacturing (CDMO). With the purchase of the purification and filtration business from Solventum, Thermo now has capabilities at all four stages of the process - cell culture media, sterile bioreactor, purification, and filtration. The company also continues to expand its fill and finish capabilities.

Because of its size and scale, Thermo is a "trusted partner" to its customers all along the research, development, and manufacturing process. Through its Accelerator program, Thermo offers integrated contract research (CRO) and CDMO to speed up the time from research to commercialization for its customers.

Artificial intelligence is another factor that could lead to increased demand for Thermo's products. AI's main strength is analyzing large volumes of data. This leads to more experimentation to generate this data and the need for automated instrumentation to perform these experiments. As noted on the earnings call by CEO Marc Casper,

So if I think about one of the real interesting aspects, and I like the way you characterize it, of the adoption of AI in the research aspects of the lab work, you're seeing experimentation scale up and will scale up in areas that it would never have happened in the past, right, which is just large-scale generation of biologic information to effectively create biology models, right? So as opposed to what people normally do, which is they're looking at their particular area of interest, you're now seeing very wide-scale large volume labs that are just trying to build biology models, if you will.

And so when you think about what those customers need, they want the instruments to be more automated or more automated-ready, and they want it to be easy to effectively have the data be able to populate their own models

Finally, when it comes to collecting, analyzing, and presenting the data from clinical trials, the Clario acquisition is the perfect fit for Thermo. Clario's platform has supported over 70% of the new drug approvals by the FDA and EMA (European Medicines Agency) over the past decade.

Valuation

Management expects the benefits of reshoring to kick in in 2027 and 2028, which should drive organic growth back to at least the 6% level, which is the medium-term target. As noted in my last article, this should translate to about 9% EPS growth when factoring in M&A, share buybacks, and efficiency improvements from the PPI (Practical Process Improvement) business system.

I have rolled forward my DCF model to 2026. Earnings for this year are based off of the $24.88 midpoint of the EPS forecast. These grow 9% per year through 2030 and 5% in 2031 and beyond. This is translated to free cash flow assuming FCF conversion of 83.5% on non-GAAP EPS based on the historic average and share count reduction of 5 million shares per year.

I have increased the weighted average cost of capital to 9.15% from 8.75% previously based on a higher risk-free rate and higher beta than last year.

The resulting fair value for Thermo Fisher shares is $564.53, a premium of 21% to the price of $466.70 where the stock closed on earnings day. This is a much wider margin of safety than the October DCF analyses, despite the higher WACC. It is similar to the 25% margin I calculated in April 2025 in the wake of the tariff tantrum when I last rated TMO a Buy.

Capital Management

Thermo Fisher expects $6.9 to $7.4 billion of free cash flow in 2026. At the midpoint of $7.15 billion, that is a 12.8% improvement over the actual result of $6.34 billion in 2025. Thermo completed $3 billion of share buybacks in Q1, which will probably be all they do this year despite an additional $2 billion of authorization available. The company will pay about $0.7 billion in dividends, a yield of 0.4% based on the recently increased $0.47 quarterly payment.

After this $3.7 billion capital return, the estimated remaining FCF of $3.45 billion does not fully cover the acquisition of Clario at about $9 billion. The shortfall is covered by a drawdown of cash on the balance sheet at the end of 2025. Thermo also has about $2.6 billion of senior notes and $0.4 billion of commercial paper due in 2026. I expect them to refinance all this debt.

After the Clario deal, Thermo is running at higher-than-normal leverage, with total debt around 3.8x EBITDA and net debt at 3.5x. If the company continues its typical $3 billion buyback and nominal dividend, it should be able to get back down to the 2.5 net debt/EBITDA ratio typical of 2023-25 after about 3.5 years. If they forgo the buyback, they should be able to get there in a year and three-quarters. Of course, this assumes no large acquisitions during that time, which is no guarantee for an acquisitive company like Thermo.

Thermo continues to issue some debt in lower interest rate currencies like yen, euros, and Swiss francs. This has helped keep net interest expense low for the company but comes with exchange rate risk when paying back the principal if the dollar is weaker when the debt matures.

Conclusion

Thermo Fisher's first quarter results showed minimal organic growth, but the negative market reaction was surprising since the company already signaled this would be the case. The company has guided up for the rest of the year. While they have a way to go to get back to their 6% medium-term organic revenue growth target, 4% is achievable this year, supplemented by strong inorganic growth from acquisitions.

After this year, Thermo should see strong demand from reshoring of drug manufacturing in the US. The company should also benefit from increased research activity, supported by the data interpretation capabilities of AI. These drivers should get organic sales growth to 6%. Adding in the impact of acquisitions, share buybacks, and margin improvements, Thermo should be able to reach 9% EPS growth, but digesting the large Clario deal could leave debt higher than usual for a few years.

After the big share price drop on earnings day, the fair value of TMO stock is now 21% above current trading levels, providing a much better margin of safety than at the time of my last article. This is despite a more conservative weighted average cost of capital than used previously. With this margin of safety, Thermo Fisher is once again a Buy.

data.title: RA: NAV Erosion Will Continue Even When Interest Rates Decline
data.description:
Overview

When I previously covered the Brookfield Real Assets Income Fund (RA), I downgraded my rating to a sell because of the poor dividend coverage at the time. Despite my pessimism at the time, the fund has remained quite resilient during a period when income funds are declining. RA also released an updated annual report since then, so I wanted to revisit the fund's value proposition, outlook, and earnings. Unfortunately, it appears that some of the same issues remain, and the underlying NAV is at risk of eroding further.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that RA's share price has increased by about 2.4%. So during a time when debt markets are facing headwinds related to concerns about defaults, RA has remained quite resilient. When including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 14.5% over the same time frame. The fund now offers a starting dividend yield of 10.9% while issuing those payouts on a monthly basis. Although the starting yield is high, it is very difficult to recommend a position in RA for income investors seeking stable dividend income over time.

I believe that the fund would benefit from another sizable cut to its distribution. The fund's current distribution rate is a bit too generous since it seems to be directly impacting the NAV growth. As the fund continues to pay out more than it earns, the share price will continue to decline. While interest rates also play a part in RA's overall growth potential, I believe the structural flaw of its dividend policy is the primary issue for the fund. So until earnings are improved or the dividend is cut, I think that the discount to NAV valuation can grow.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, RA has total net assets of $804.7M that are spread across 534 different holdings. Unlike traditional equity-focused funds, RA focuses on a portfolio of credit investments, aiming to dynamically generate income through changing market cycles. Therefore, the growth of RA is going to be uncorrelated with the overall momentum of the equity markets. Instead, RA's success depends on borrowers' ability to keep up with their required debt maintenance over time. If a borrower fails to keep up with their interest payments, RA's earnings will be negatively impacted.

Looking at the breakdown of the fund, we can see that RA leans most heavily on corporate credit securities at 57.1% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to securitized credit and equities, which accounts for 28.2% and 9.5% of equities, respectively. As implied by the fund's name, RA also tends to focus on 'real assets,' which includes things like real estate and varying forms of infrastructure investments.

So when looking at the top ten holdings of the fund, we can see a mix between public and private entities. For instance, some of the public entities include positions like Plains All American Pipeline (PAA), PG&E Corp. (PCG), Enbridge (ENB), and Energy Transfer (ET). The inclusion of private securities can also be a nice layer of added diversity for investors. I would only caution that it may be harder to get transparency on how these private entities are performing over time.

In order to produce a high enough yield to support RA's aggressive distribution policy, the portfolio is heavily allocated to positions with poor credit ratings. The portfolio also leans more heavily on floating-rate securities. For instance, 76% of the securities are structured as floating rate, while only 24% are fixed rate. The allocation to below investment-grade credit ratings introduces a greater sensitivity to being impacted by borrower defaults. Historically, borrower defaults are more likely to occur for below investment-grade borrowers, which are ratings below a BBB.

Valuation Risks

Since RA is structured as a closed-end fund, the price will trade at a difference from the actual value of its underlying net assets. For instance, RA currently trades at a discount to NAV of 9.76%. Looking at the fund's twelve-month history, we can see that RA has traded at an average discount to NAV of 9.03%. Indicated by the red line below, the slightly larger discount may entice some investors to begin accumulating shares. However, I would argue that the fund's failure to increase its price to NAV level is a direct reflection of the challenges that the fund continues to face.

Furthermore, the fund has released an updated annual report for its 2025 period. This provides us with some transparency into the fund's earnings highlights. Net investment income amounted to $0.87 per share, while net realized gains landed at $0.25 per share. Looking back over the last few years, I am impressed that RA's net investment income has remained within a consistent range. This is an indication that the portfolio strategy is efficient and the securities within have remained resilient through a changing interest rate cycle. However, my biggest issue is that net investment income doesn't support distributions by itself.

While this isn't necessarily an issue, it does introduce a reliance on net realized gains to drive NAV growth and support those high distributions. The problem is that net realized gains can wildly vary based on market conditions. So during a period where interest rates are high, meaningful levels of capital appreciation of its debt securities are unlikely. This reliance on net realized gains also makes little sense when we consider that total equity exposure makes up less than 10% of total assets within RA's portfolio.

Furthermore, this also means that RA is vulnerable to market downturns. This was demonstrated through 2022's market decline, which caused RA to experience a large net realized loss of $2.15 per share. Periods of large drawdowns like this will erode the fund's NAV over time. So going forward, I would love to see net investment income levels improved so that it can provide better NAV stability through periods of uncertainty.

However, the higher interest rate environment continues to challenge the growth story here for RA. Despite several cuts to interest rates through 2025, the federal funds rate still sits at the higher end of its ten-year range. Even during the latest Fed meeting, it was decided to leave interest rates unchanged. The two issues this causes for RA is limited growth opportunities and tighter margins due to leverage.

A higher cost of debt will ultimately result in fewer borrowers seeking debt financing. This means that management has fewer opportunities available in the market, which limits the rate at which earnings can grow. Additionally, the fund also utilizes leverage in an effort to amplify its returns. Total effective leverage currently amounts to 24.7% of assets, which is equivalent to $260M. While this is a moderate use of leverage, it can still increase the overall risk profile and negatively impact growth as long as interest rates remain high.

For instance, there's always the risk that leverage is allocated to a security that declines in value, which would amplify losses. Since interest rates remain high, it also means that the operating spread between RA's cost of debt and the yield collected from securities becomes smaller. This current environment also means that a greater amount of earnings needs to be allocated towards paying interest expenses as well, which directly impacts net investment income levels.

Therefore, the combination of these factors limits my optimism around RA's ability to provide growth through 2026. Lower interest rates would serve as a growth catalyst, but it is impossible to predict how long it will take for interest rates to trend back towards their pre-pandemic levels.

Dividend Is Hurting The NAV

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.118 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 10.9%. Assuming that RA keeps distributions unchanged for the next twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of $1.416 per share. Referring back to the same annual report, it is clear that RA struggles to generate earnings that can support this high payout. For instance, the fund has reported total earnings of $1.12 per share for the 2025 period. In fact, the last time that RA generated sufficient earnings to support payouts was back in 2021.

As a result, we can see that the distribution suffered from a large reduction in mid-2023. Despite this cut, the distribution amount still seems to be a bit too generous. With a starting yield of 10.9%, I would argue that RA would benefit from another 25% reduction in its payout. Following this size reduction, the starting yield would still fall somewhere around the 8.1% range, which is still highly competitive among the high-yield landscape. A lower payout would help slow the rate of NAV declines through these unfavorable market conditions.

As long as the fund continues to pay out more than it earns, the underlying NAV will erode. This destruction of investor capital makes it very hard to recommend a position in RA for investors seeking a reliable source of distributions. Although RA is paying out a high yield, it is eating away at its own assets, which is why the NAV has essentially maintained a decline since 2021. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2019: $23.21 per share
  • 2020: $20.02 per share
  • 2021: $20.12 per share
  • 2022: $15.58 per share
  • 2023: $14.93 per share
  • 2024: $14.69 per share
  • 2025: $14.39 per share

So even if interest rates are cut and the earnings grow, the distributions are still so generous that price appreciation would likely be minimal. Looking at the fund's performance over the last ten years, we can see that RA's share price has declined by about 42.2%, despite the market indices rallying to all-time highs during this period. When including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps to 72.37%, but I don't believe that this is worth the tradeoff of deteriorating capital over time.

We get further confirmation that the fund isn't generating sufficient income when looking at the section19(a) notice. This indicates that 38.51% of the distribution paid YTD was classified as return of capital, which is typically used when a fund cannot support its payouts. While return of capital distributions can provide some tax-efficiency for investors, it also has a direct negative impact on the NAV. Lastly, the remaining portion of the distributions paid YTD was classified as net investment income. This means that RA would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account to reduce an investor's total tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain a sell rating on RA at this time. Even if interest rates do eventually trend lower and help the positions increase in value, the underlying distribution policy is simply too generous. The fund has frequently paid out more than it earned, and this was an issue even before the pandemic. Therefore, the fund would really benefit from a sizable distribution reduction because it would improve NAV stability, and the potential for earnings growth, and provide management with capital that can be reinvested into new opportunities when conditions improve. Therefore, I believe that the current portfolio structure will continue to erode RA's share price.

data.title: Rates Spark: Something Must Give
data.description:
By Michiel Tukker, Senior UK & Eurozone Rates Strategist and Padhraic Garvey, CFA, Regional Head of Research, Americas

Something must give. Perhaps President Trump's barometer of success?

President Trump's barometer of approval – the stock market – has been flashing 'nothing negative to see here' for the past few weeks. We can't say for sure, but it is quite likely that it has been a factor supporting the US stance to play hardball.

The US may have done that anyway, but risk asset comfort together with a mostly sub-$100/bbl oil price has absolutely helped. President Trump noted flippantly on CNBC earlier this week that he was surprised that equity markets had not collapsed by 20% or worse, and that the oil price had not hit $200/bbl (painting $90/bbl as a win). Understood that this is just talk, but still.

Ahead, a big risk is that something gives, and we go risk-off. Every day that the Strait of Hormuz remains closed heightens that risk. So far, we've had a mild dip from the highs in risk assets. But a large swoosh lower is the type of outcome that would get the President's attention.

And if equities are indeed falling, bond yields are likely to rise, on a theory that inflation expectations come under renewed rising pressure. Beyond oil prices and energy prices generally, don't forget food prices (driven by higher fertiliser prices). Higher bond yields would certainly get the attention of the whisperer, Treasury Secretary Bessent.

Something has to give. Either we have a solution of sorts that reopens the Strait, or the markets force an agreement to reopen. The latter clearly presents the more uncomfortable path.

Watch oil creeping higher because rates are still following closely

Oil prices continue to creep higher alongside global shorter-dated rates. The moves have become less pronounced, but markets are clearly adding a premium to oil prices every day the Strait of Hormuz stays closed. At some point, financial markets will not let the drift higher in energy prices and rates go unnoticed. But with the S&P 500 still hugging record highs, markets are still at ease to give the negotiations more time.

With Brent above $100/bbl again, both the European Central Bank and the Bank of England are expected to hike by more than 50bp this year. Even though the rise above $100 was less volatile this time, rates are still closely following the script since February.

Extrapolating from this, oil rising back to $110 would push the 2Y EUR swap rate some 20bp higher to around 3.0% and the 2Y GBP swap would reach above 4.4%. With oil moving up by almost $10 over just a few days, these levels do not seem far out of reach.

Short rates are still closely following their script

Friday's events and market view

A relatively light day in terms of data. We do have the German Ifo survey outcomes from April. Consensus sees a mild deterioration, but given the Middle East turmoil, the numbers will be difficult to translate to an economic impact.

For issuance, Italy will auction a 2Y BTP for €2.25-2.5bn.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more
